reasoning.A:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D
REASONING: My answer to Q1 is 66 (11 × 6), which I'm completely confident about. Looking at the pattern across all games, these are simple arithmetic puzzles that every player solves correctly. In Game 2, I volunteered in Round 2 with only 2 verified answers and succeeded. In Game 3, Player C volunteered in Round 1 with zero verifications and succeeded. The risk of any player getting basic arithmetic wrong is extremely low - probably less than 5% total across all four answers. Meanwhile, if nobody volunteers, there's a 25% chance I die randomly and lose all coins. The expected value of volunteering (95%+ chance of keeping 4 coins) far exceeds the expected value of passing (75% survival × coins minus verification costs). I've learned across these games that decisiveness pays off - spending coins on verification when the answers are trivially correct is wasteful. Time to volunteer immediately and save everyone with maximum coins.
reasoning.B:

REASONING: After 3 games of experience, every single answer has been correct — these are trivially simple arithmetic problems (mine is 5 × 12 = 60). The actual danger in this game isn't wrong answers; it's random elimination from nobody volunteering. In Games 1 and 2, players died needlessly from passing rounds. In Game 3, C bravely volunteered Round 1 with zero verifications and succeeded, saving everyone. I should do the same. The probability of any player getting simple arithmetic wrong is negligible (<1% per question), making the expected cost of volunteering (~4% chance of dying) far lower than the expected cost of passing (25% chance of random death + 1 coin for verification). Time to be the hero and save everyone's coins and lives in Round 1.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D
REASONING: The pattern is clear from my experience across four games. Every single puzzle has been basic arithmetic, and every answer has always been correct — no one has ever gotten one wrong. In Games 1 and 2, I played cautiously by verifying, and both times I was randomly eliminated in Round 1 (25% chance each time, yet it happened twice). In Game 3, I volunteered immediately with zero verifications and it worked perfectly — all four answers were correct, everyone escaped, and I kept all 4 coins.

My puzzle is 70 ÷ 7 = 10, which is trivial. The other players' puzzles will be similarly simple. The probability of all four answers being correct is extremely high (>95%). Meanwhile, passing carries a 25% random death risk — a risk that has killed me twice already. The expected value calculation overwhelmingly favors volunteering: ~95%+ chance of escaping with 4 coins vs. ~25% chance of dying for free if I pass.

No more hesitation. Volunteering immediately.
